questions.to_find: Which expression calls f(int, short) without a type checking error

understand.plain: The function f expects two arguments: first an int and second a short. We have an int variable i, a short variable s, and a short pointer p pointing to s. We need to find which call expression matches the expected types exactly (or with implicit promotion that is safe).

keywords.explain: The compiler verifies that each argument passed to a function matches or is implicitly convertible to the declared parameter type.
keywords.example: Passing a short* where a short is expected is a type error.

keywords.explain: Using * before a pointer variable gives the value stored at that address; *p where p is short* gives a short value.
keywords.example: If p = &s where s is short, then *p yields the short value 12.

understand.visual_alt: Function f takes (int, short). Variable i is int, s is short, p is short* pointing to s. Dereferencing *p gives short.

given.aim: Find the call expression that passes correct types to f(int, short)

terms.meaning: Function f expects 1st argument as int and 2nd argument as short
terms.example: f(10, 5) would be valid if 10 is int and 5 is short
terms.connects: Defines the required parameter types we must match

terms.meaning: i is an int variable with value 100
terms.example: passing i as 1st argument to f satisfies the int requirement
terms.connects: Can directly satisfy the first int parameter

terms.meaning: s is a short; p is a pointer to short storing address of s
terms.example: *p dereferences to give short value 12, same as s
terms.connects: *p can satisfy the second short parameter

given.plan: Check each option: verify that the 1st argument is int (or int-compatible) and the 2nd argument is short (or short-compatible). Eliminate options where argument types are mismatched or where illegal operations are used.

steps.title: Eliminate option A: f(i, *s)
steps.apply: s is a short variable, NOT a pointer. Applying the dereference operator * to a non-pointer type s is a compile-time type error. So f(i, *s) is invalid.
steps.note: *s is illegal because s is not a pointer type.

steps.title: Eliminate option B: f(*p, s)
steps.apply: *p dereferences the short pointer p to give a short value (12). The first parameter of f requires int, but *p is short. Although short can be implicitly promoted to int in many contexts, the 2nd argument s is short which matches the 2nd parameter, so the 2nd param is fine. However, 1st arg *p is short but f expects int — this triggers a type checking warning/error. Hence B is not the best answer.
steps.note: In strict type checking, passing short where int is expected is a type mismatch.

steps.title: Verify option C: f(i, *p)
steps.apply: i is int — matches 1st parameter (int). *p is the value at the short pointer p, which is a short — matches 2nd parameter (short). Both types match exactly. No type checking error occurs.
steps.note: This is the only option where both argument types exactly match the declared parameter types.

steps.title: Eliminate option D: f(i, p)
steps.apply: i is int — matches 1st parameter. p is short* (a pointer to short), but the 2nd parameter requires short. Passing a pointer where a scalar short is expected is a type error.
steps.note: short* is not the same as short; pointer type cannot be passed as scalar.

solution.result: C. f(i, *p) — i is int (matches 1st param) and *p is short (matches 2nd param), so no type checking error occurs.

questions.formula_note: C function call type checking: each argument must match the declared parameter type; dereferencing a short* yields short.

questions.to_find: The loop condition that makes the function correctly compute the maximum element

understand.plain: The algorithm uses two indices a (starting from left = 0) and b (starting from right = n-1). At each step it discards the smaller end: if p[a] <= p[b] then the left end is not maximum so move a forward; otherwise the right end is not maximum so move b backward. When a == b both indices point to the same element, which must be the maximum.

keywords.explain: Two indices start at opposite ends of the array and move toward each other, discarding elements that cannot be the maximum.
keywords.example: For [3,1,4,1,5], a starts at 0 (value 3) and b at 4 (value 5). Since p[0]<=p[4], a moves to 1. Eventually both converge on the max.

keywords.explain: At every iteration, the maximum of the array lies within p[a..b]. The loop shrinks this range until a==b.
keywords.example: After each step, one endpoint that cannot be max is eliminated.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 500 120"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="30" width="70" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="45" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">3</text><rect x="90" y="30" width="70" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="125" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">1</text><rect x="170" y="30" width="70" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="205" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">4</text><rect x="250" y="30" width="70" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="285" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">1</text><rect x="330" y="30" width="70" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="365" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">5</text><text x="45" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ok">a</text><text x="365" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="tag no">b</text><text x="250" y="15" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">a and b converge toward the max element</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array [3,1,4,1,5] with index a=0 pointing to 3 on the left and b=4 pointing to 5 on the right. They converge toward the maximum.

given.aim: Determine the while-loop condition that terminates correctly when both indices meet at the maximum

terms.meaning: a is the left index starting at 0, b is the right index starting at last element
terms.connects: They converge toward each other; loop must run while a != b

terms.meaning: Discard the smaller endpoint: advance a if left is smaller-or-equal, else retreat b
terms.example: If p[0]=3 <= p[4]=5, then a becomes 1, narrowing search to p[1..4]
terms.connects: Each iteration discards one element that cannot be the global max

terms.meaning: When loop ends, a and b should both point to the maximum element
terms.example: When a==b, p[a] is the only surviving candidate, which is the max
terms.connects: The loop must end exactly when a==b to return the correct max

given.plan: The algorithm is correct when a != b (i.e., loop continues while there is still a range to examine). When a == b, one element remains in the window and it is the maximum. So the missing condition is a != b.

steps.title: Trace the algorithm with a small example
steps.apply:
Let p = [3, 1, 5, 2, 4], n=5, so a=0, b=4.
Iteration 1: p[0]=3 <= p[4]=4, so a++ -> a=1.
Iteration 2: p[1]=1 <= p[4]=4, so a++ -> a=2.
Iteration 3: p[2]=5 > p[4]=4, so b-- -> b=3.
Iteration 4: p[2]=5 > p[3]=2, so b-- -> b=2.
Now a=2, b=2: a == b, loop ends. Return p[2]=5. Correct!
steps.note: The loop runs while a != b; once a==b the single remaining index is the maximum.

steps.title: Verify options B, C, D are wrong
steps.apply:
B. b != 0: loop would stop when b reaches 0, which is wrong if max is at index 0.
C. a <= n/2: loop stops prematurely when a reaches middle, missing elements in right half.
D. b != a+1: loop would stop one iteration too early (when b=a+1 there are still 2 elements to compare).
steps.note: Only a != b is the correct termination condition that ensures both indices converge to the maximum.

solution.result: A. a != b — the loop continues as long as the two indices have not converged, and returns p[a] (== p[b]) which is the maximum.

questions.formula_note: Two-pointer convergence algorithm: maintain window [a,b] discarding non-maximum endpoints until a==b.

questions.to_find: The output printed by the program

understand.plain: The key trap here is that strlen returns size_t which is an unsigned type. When we compute strlen(s) - strlen(t) where strlen("abc")=3 and strlen("defgh")=5, in ordinary arithmetic this gives -2. But since both are unsigned, the subtraction wraps around to a very large positive number (unsigned underflow). Comparing this large positive number to c=0 gives true, so len = strlen(s) = 3.

keywords.explain: strlen returns size_t, an unsigned integer type. Subtracting a larger unsigned value from a smaller one does not give a negative result; instead it wraps around to a large positive value.
When arithmetic on unsigned integers produces a mathematically negative result, the value wraps around modulo 2^32 (or 2^64), yielding a large positive number.

understand.visual_alt: strlen("abc")=3 (unsigned), strlen("defgh")=5 (unsigned). Subtraction 3-5 unsigned wraps to ~4294967294. This is > 0 (c=0), so ternary picks strlen(s)=3. Output: 3.

given.aim: Determine what value len takes and what the program prints

terms.meaning: s = "abc" has length 3; t = "defgh" has length 5. Both return size_t (unsigned int).
terms.connects: The subtraction 3u - 5u is done in unsigned arithmetic

terms.meaning: c is an unsigned int initialized to 0, used as the comparison threshold
terms.connects: The expression (strlen(s)-strlen(t)) > c is evaluated in unsigned arithmetic because both operands are unsigned

terms.meaning: If (strlen(s)-strlen(t)) > c is true, len = strlen(s); otherwise len = strlen(t)
terms.example: If the unsigned difference is large (> 0), len = strlen(s) = 3
terms.connects: Due to unsigned wraparound, the condition is true, so len = 3

given.plan: Compute strlen(s)=3 and strlen(t)=5 as unsigned values. Subtract to get 3u-5u which wraps to a large unsigned number (> 0u = c). Therefore the ternary picks strlen(s)=3. The printf with %d prints 3.

steps.title: Compute string lengths
steps.apply:
strlen(s) = strlen("abc") = 3 (type: unsigned int)
strlen(t) = strlen("defgh") = 5 (type: unsigned int)
steps.note: The return type is unsigned, which is crucial for the next step.

steps.title: Evaluate the subtraction in unsigned arithmetic
steps.apply: strlen(s) - strlen(t) = 3u - 5u. Since both are unsigned, this wraps around: result = UINT_MAX - 1 = 4294967294 (a very large positive number), NOT -2.
steps.note: This is the classic unsigned underflow trap. The mathematical result -2 is represented as ~4294967294 in unsigned 32-bit.

steps.title: Evaluate the condition
steps.apply: c = 0 (unsigned int). The comparison is 4294967294u > 0u which is TRUE. So the ternary expression evaluates to strlen(s) = 3.
steps.note: Had the subtraction been signed, result would be -2, and -2 > 0 would be FALSE giving strlen(t)=5. But unsigned changes the outcome.

steps.title: Print the result
steps.apply: len = 3 (int). printf("%d\n", 3) prints 3 followed by a newline.

solution.result: 3 — due to unsigned integer underflow in the subtraction, the condition evaluates to true and len = strlen("abc") = 3.

questions.formula_note: Unsigned integer arithmetic: subtracting a larger unsigned value from a smaller one wraps around (modulo 2^32 or 2^64), never giving a negative result — a common C gotcha with size_t comparisons.
